project_overview.project_name: Honduras Tropical Cyclones Eta and Iota Emergency Recovery Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To support Honduras' response and recovery needs and strengthen institutional capacity to manage a resilient and inclusive recovery and reconstruction
project_overview.beneficiaries: At least 800,000 inhabitants in affected communities, approximately 50% women, including Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants; Government officials and local authorities

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on most affected regions
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Honduras faces crime and gang violence challenges; affected areas include territories of Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants; country experiences high vulnerability to natural disasters and climate shocks

key_components.component_name: Emergency Relief, Response and Public Health and Safety Operations
key_components.description: Financing of immediate relief and response measures including emergency evacuation and rescue, renting of private transport equipment, distribution of food/non-food items and PPE, sheltering, water and debris clearing, and disease outbreak prevention measures

key_components.component_name: Resilient Rehabilitation and Reconstruction of Public and Community Infrastructure and Resumption of Public Services
key_components.description: Technical preparation and implementation of urgent repairs and resilient rehabilitation/reconstruction of high-priority public and community infrastructure including roads, bridges, public buildings (schools, health facilities, government offices, first responder facilities), with focus on building-back-better approach incorporating climate resilience and disaster risk mitigation

key_components.component_name: Institutional Support to the Government Recovery and Reconstruction Program
key_components.description: Financing of Project Implementation Unit operations, project management, procurement, financial management, M&E, technical audits, compliance monitoring, grievance redress mechanism, capacity building for SEDECOAS and other DRM agencies, asset management approaches, and communication strategy

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: SEDECOAS (Secretaría de Desarrollo Comunitario, Agua y Saneamiento - Ministry of Community Development, Water and Sanitation) through a dedicated Project Implementation Unit (PIU)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Flexible institutional coordination mechanism between SEDECOAS and technical support ministries; coordination with Municipal Emergency Committees (CODEM) and Local Emergency Committees (CODELES); inter-institutional agreements with participating entities; coordination with COPECO (National Disaster Risk Management Agency); participation of local authorities and IP/AD organizations in prioritization processes
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Fast-track emergency procurement including direct contracting, streamlined competitive procedures with shorter bidding times, use of government electronic catalog and framework agreements, procurement from UN Agencies; advance procurement and retroactive financing allowed; procurement risk rated as High
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PIU responsible for overall M&E and regular reporting to World Bank; bi-annual progress reports; collection of baseline data for social, environmental and economic impacts; outcomes and intermediate level indicators tracked; citizen satisfaction surveys; grievance monitoring; external audits (bi-annual technical, financial and system audits)

identified_risks.risk_description: Political polarization has traditionally delayed approvals of internationally financed projects; political changes may result in deviations from key objectives
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong consultation and information provided to all stakeholders on national reconstruction plan; broad understanding across political actors of importance and urgency of reconstruction efforts

identified_risks.risk_description: External shocks (extreme weather, global economic slowdown, lower agricultural prices), domestic factors (pandemic impact, fiscal instability, ENEE deficit, weakened tax revenues), uncertainty about post-pandemic recovery speed
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implementation of Disaster Risk Financing Strategy by SEFIN; measures supported by Cat DDO to promote climate resilience and provide fiscal buffer; IMF program supporting fiscal sustainability reforms

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: New PIU needs to be created and staffed with qualified experts; limited capacity of SEDECOAS due to COVID-19 and hurricane emergencies; need for coordination among multiple ministries
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project finances PIU operating expenses and additional technical staff; inputs from relevant line ministries for technical quality; Bank provides training and guidance; leverage expertise from other Bank-funded projects in Honduras; extension of DRM Project (P131094) capacity to support early implementation

identified_risks.risk_description: Emergency nature of operation; lack of established processes at SEDECOAS/PIU; capacity challenges for FM and procurement functions given project complexity and size; need for robust fiduciary environment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Dedicated FM specialist with knowledge of GoH finance systems; FM arrangements and disbursements detailed in POM; fast-track disbursement arrangements; integration with GoH's Integrated Financial Management Information System (SIAFI-UEPEX); external independent audit firm contracted; bi-annual technical, financial and system audits; clear roles and responsibilities between ministries

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential environmental impacts from debris clearing, earthworks, civil works, waste generation and disposal, wastewater discharge, traffic risks, health and safety risks including COVID-19 spread, malaria, dengue; social risks including exclusion of IPs/ADs, vulnerable populations, GBV, labor influx, potential involuntary resettlement, crime and gang violence, displacement, inadequate supervision of retroactive activities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), Labor Management Procedures (LMP), Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), Indigenous Peoples and Afro-Descendants Plan (IPADP); E&S screening checklists and site-specific ESIAs/ESMPs; generic E&S management procedures and codes of practice; GBV and SEA risk management procedures; worker Code of Conduct; universal access design principles; robust Grievance Redress Mechanism; dedicated environmental, social, and health/safety specialists in PIU; community-based participatory processes; third-party monitoring for complex risks

identified_risks.risk_description: Lack of availability of goods/services/works due to increased local demand; limited implementing entity capacity; difficulty hiring qualified PIU staff quickly; governance issues in emergency contexts; conflict of interest with local vendors
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Market research and analysis to identify potential contractors; access to local and international markets; hiring of experienced procurement and technical staff; procurement via UN agencies where they have comparative advantage; due diligence on conflict of interest; clear definition of roles and responsibilities in POM; hands-on expanded implementation support from Bank

identified_risks.risk_description: Multidisciplinary nature requires coordination among multiple stakeholders; ministries and agencies have not traditionally coordinated efficiently; coordination with NGOs and humanitarian organizations challenging; pandemic limits traditional engagement channels; affected populations face difficulties accessing safe communication channels
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: SEDECOAS continues institutional coordination support; participatory approaches to investment selection; adequate operation of SEP engagement strategy; robust, inclusive and culturally adapted GRM; involvement of local authorities and IP/AD organizations in decision-making

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 pandemic (Honduras among most affected in LAC region); country prone to hurricanes and forest fires; ongoing La Niña Southern Oscillation expected to generate severe droughts in first half of 2021; occurrence of new natural disaster could undermine recovery progress
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: GoH with Bank and donor support continues strengthening DRM agencies; beneficiaries to receive training on disaster preparedness and response; building-back-better approach with improved construction standards; integration of disaster risk and climate change considerations into infrastructure designs

identified_risks.risk_description: Framework approach with significant flexibility defers details to POM and Recovery/Rehabilitation Plan; lack of detailed information on damages and losses; simplified and preliminary economic analysis
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Prioritization process during implementation based on criteria in POM; screening for eligibility; World Bank no-objection required for proposed investments; participatory and inclusive process involving local authorities and IPAD communities; climate-smart investment focus; economic analysis shows IRR of 13.45% and NPV of US$138 million at 4% discount rate

identified_risks.risk_description: Alignment with national DRM policies and frameworks; need for coordination with multiple sectoral policies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project aligned with UN Sendai Framework, Central America Policy for DRM (PCGIR), Honduras National DRM Plan, and National Emergency Operations Plan; support for operationalization of Disaster Risk Financing Strategy; alignment with World Bank CPF and COVID-19 Response Approach

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High environmental and social risk classification
triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7)
Projects on International Waterways (OP 7.50) - exception granted
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - robust GRM established to address concerns and complaints promptly and transparently with no cost or discrimination toward project-affected communities; channels developed for community M&E of site-specific activities through social audits or citizen feedback platforms; implementing staff and local-level coordination structures oriented on roles and responsibilities; World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel also available
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender-sensitive participatory processes for defining priority investments; gender-informed implementation of activities; focus on female-headed households in housing reconstruction; women targeted as 48% of beneficiaries; gender specialist in PIU; training targets 48% women; GBV risk management procedures and SEA prevention measures included in codes of conduct; recognition that disaster impacts disproportionately affect women through disruptions to health, food security, livelihoods, and increased domestic violence
vulnerable_groups: Indigenous Peoples (IPs) - 8.64% of population, 72% in extreme poverty, higher vulnerability to disaster impacts
Afro-descendants (ADs) - face similar vulnerabilities as IPs
Women - disproportionately affected by disasters, food insecurity, livelihood disruption, GBV
Persons with disabilities - universal design principles to be followed
Poor and extremely poor populations - estimated 60% of Eta/Iota affected population
Vulnerable populations as defined in SINAGER's DRM Law (Art. 10)
Groups in shelters - significant health challenges from COVID-19

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project acknowledges crime and gang violence as contextual risks that can pose threats to local communities and project workers and could hinder implementation of site-specific activities; governance-related issues common in emergency situations identified as risk; project design includes measures to ensure inclusive participation and reduce exclusion risks; participatory processes designed to be culturally sensitive and inclusive of marginalized groups; GRM designed to address grievances transparently; focus on building social cohesion through inclusive recovery
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Honduras faces multiple fragility factors: (1) High exposure to natural hazards - most severely impacted by extreme weather events 1996-2015 with annual losses of 2.1% of GDP; (2) Economic vulnerability - modest volatile growth, 50% poverty rate, 30% vulnerability; (3) Structural challenges - weak institutions, political instability, limited economic diversification, thin economic base; (4) Social vulnerabilities - Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants face extreme poverty (72%), discrimination, precarious working conditions; (5) Security challenges - crime, migration pressures; (6) Fiscal instability - ENEE deficit, weakened tax revenues; (7) External shocks - COVID-19 pandemic, La Niña southern oscillation, global economic slowdown, lower agricultural prices; (8) Compound crises - simultaneous impact of COVID-19, Eta/Iota hurricanes, and La Niña creating severe fiscal and financial system stress
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Crime and gang violence identified as substantial risk during disaster response and reconstruction; project workers and local communities face security threats; gang violence could hinder implementation of site-specific activities; emergency context creates governance challenges; project design includes measures to address security concerns through coordination mechanisms and community engagement; local authorities and CODEL/CODEM involvement in planning to enhance local security awareness
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Over 1 million people evacuated due to Eta and Iota; approximately 96,000 remain in shelters as of December 2020; significant number expected to remain in need of shelter and basic nutrition for extended period due to destruction and damage; project finances short and long-term sheltering as part of Component 1; Resettlement Policy Framework prepared to address potential exceptional cases of land acquisition or economic displacement; project explicitly states no involuntary resettlement will occur - all buildings reconstructed in situ within existing footprints or on publicly owned land; temporary land taking for contractor camps and storage may occur; potential relocation of encroachers and interruption to adjacent business access identified as possible scenarios
